questions.prompt: What's the key benefit of `layout.tsx` over rendering shared UI in each page?

choices.text: Layouts persist across client-side navigations between sibling routes — they don't re-mount, don't re-fetch, and preserve state. Going from /dashboard to /dashboard/settings keeps the sidebar's accordion state, form input, and scroll position; only the page content swaps. Sharing UI by importing it into every page would re-render and re-fetch every navigation. Layouts compose by directory nesting; each layout wraps everything below it.

questions.explanation: Layouts persist across sibling navigations — no re-mount, state preserved.

questions.prompt: Need an animation that runs on every page transition. layout or template?

choices.text: template.tsx. Templates work like layouts but re-mount on every navigation, firing useEffect and animation lifecycles each transition. Layouts persist across navigations, so an animation defined in a layout's mount effect only fires once. Use templates sparingly — they're the right tool for per-navigation animations or per-navigation state resets, but they sacrifice the state-preservation win that makes layouts so useful. For 95% of cases, layouts are correct; templates are a specialized escape hatch.

questions.explanation: template.tsx re-mounts on each navigation; right for per-transition animations.
